title: Rotate array right by k
description: Rotate the array to the right by k steps

examples.explanation:
Initial: [1,2,3,4,5]
Rotation 1: Store last=5, shift right: [1,1,2,3,4] -> place 5 at index 0: [5,1,2,3,4]
Rotation 2: Store last=4, shift right: [5,5,1,2,3] -> place 4 at index 0: [4,5,1,2,3]
Final: [4,5,1,2,3]

k = 4, n = 3, effective k = 4 % 3 = 1
Rotation 1: Store last=30, shift right: [10,10,20] -> place 30 at index 0: [30,10,20]
Final: [30,10,20]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*k)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly shift the array right by one step for k times, moving the last element to the front each time. Use this only for small inputs due to inefficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate Array Right by k Steps Using Brute Force O(n*k)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of rotating an array right by k steps using repeated one-step rotations.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the rotation problem through repeated single-step rotations. The intuition is simple: rotating right by k steps is equivalent to performing k individual right rotations, where each rotation moves the last element to the front and shifts all other elements right by one position. This works because each single rotation preserves the relative order of elements while moving the last element to the front. The algorithm starts by handling edge cases: if k is 0 or the array is empty, return immediately. For k rotations, we repeatedly: store the last element in a temporary variable, shift all elements from index 1 to n-1 right by one position (using a backward loop to avoid overwriting), then place the temporary element at index 0. The time complexity is O(n*k) because for each of k rotations, we traverse n elements to shift them. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only constant extra space. This approach connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) by executing it k times. Common mistakes include not handling k > n (use k % n to reduce rotations), using forward shifting which overwrites elements, and forgetting edge cases. The real pattern involves nested loops: outer for k iterations, inner for shifting.

examples.trace:
Initial: [1,2,3,4,5]
Rotation 1: Store last=5, shift right: [1,1,2,3,4] -> place 5 at index 0: [5,1,2,3,4]
Rotation 2: Store last=4, shift right: [5,5,1,2,3] -> place 4 at index 0: [4,5,1,2,3]
Final: [4,5,1,2,3]

k = 4, n = 3, effective k = 4 % 3 = 1
Rotation 1: Store last=30, shift right: [10,10,20] -> place 30 at index 0: [30,10,20]
Final: [30,10,20]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rotate Array Right by One Step
ladders.desc: Implement a single right rotation that moves the last element to the front.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs one right rotation on an array. The intuition is that rotating right by one step requires preserving all elements while repositioning the last element to the front. This works because in a right rotation, each element moves one position to the right, with the last element wrapping around to the first position. The algorithm: first, store the last element in a temporary variable to prevent loss. Then, shift all elements from index 1 to n-1 right by one position; this must be done in reverse order (from last to second element) to avoid overwriting values. Finally, assign the temporary element to index 0. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the array once for shifting. Space complexity is O(1) using only one extra variable. This feeds into L0 by being called k times for full rotation. Common mistakes include shifting in forward order (which overwrites), not handling empty arrays, and incorrect indexing. The real pattern uses a backward loop for shifting.

examples.trace:
Store last=4
Shift right (backward from index 3 to 1):
Index 3: arr[3] = arr[2] -> [1,2,3,3]
Index 2: arr[2] = arr[1] -> [1,2,2,3]
Index 1: arr[1] = arr[0] -> [1,1,2,3]
Place 4 at index 0: [4,1,2,3]

Store last=20
Shift right: index 1: arr[1] = arr[0] -> [10,10]
Place 20 at index 0: [20,10]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Shifting Array Elements Right by One Position
ladders.desc: Shift all elements in an array one position to the right, handling the wrap-around manually.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the shifting operation within a single rotation. The intuition is that to move elements right, we need to reassign each element to its next index, but we must do this carefully to avoid data loss. This works because in a right shift, element at index i moves to index i+1, except for the last element which is handled separately. The algorithm: iterate from the last index down to the second index (i from n-1 to 1), and set arr[i] = arr[i-1]. This backward traversal ensures we don't overwrite values needed later. After shifting, the original first element is duplicated at index 1, and the last element is overwritten (but saved earlier). Time complexity is O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the main step after saving the last element. Common mistakes include forward iteration (which corrupts data), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, and not considering empty arrays. The real pattern is a simple for loop in reverse.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Array Indices in Reverse Order
ladders.desc: Traverse an array from the last index to the first, accessing each element.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through an array in reverse order, which is essential for the shifting operation to prevent overwriting. The intuition is that when modifying an array in-place, processing elements from end to start ensures that values needed for future computations are not lost. This works because in a right shift, we assign arr[i] = arr[i-1]; if we go forward, we overwrite arr[i] before it's used as arr[i-1] in the next step. The algorithm: use a loop with index i starting from n-1 down to a specified lower bound (e.g., 1), decrementing by 1 each time. Within the loop, access arr[i] and arr[i-1] as needed. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the mechanism for safe shifting. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one), using forward iteration, and not handling empty arrays. The real pattern is a for loop with range(n-1, 0, -1) in Python-style.

examples.trace:
Start i=3: access arr[3]=4 and arr[2]=3
i=2: access arr[2]=3 and arr[1]=2
i=1: access arr[1]=2 and arr[0]=1
End

Start i=1: access arr[1]=20 and arr[0]=10
End

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using indices to manipulate array elements, which is fundamental for in-place algorithms. The intuition is that arrays provide direct access to elements via integer indices, allowing efficient updates. This works because arrays are contiguous memory structures where each element can be accessed in O(1) time using its index. The algorithm: to read an element, use arr[i]; to write, use arr[i] = value. Ensure indices are within bounds (0 to n-1). Time complexity for access/modification is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) per operation. This connects to L3 as the action performed during iteration. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, confusing zero-based indexing, and not updating correctly. The real pattern is simple assignment and retrieval.

examples.input: arr = [10,20,30], read index 1, write index 2 with 40
examples.trace:
Read arr[1] = 20
Write arr[2] = 40: array updates to [10,20,40]

examples.input: arr = [5], read index 0, write index 0 with 6
examples.trace:
Read arr[0] = 5
Write arr[0] = 6: array updates to [6]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces arrays, a basic data structure, and indexing. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each identifiable by an index. The intuition is that arrays allow organized storage and quick access to data. This works because indices start at 0 and go up to n-1 for an array of size n, providing a direct mapping to memory addresses. Key concepts: arrays have fixed size (in many languages), elements are of the same type, and access via arr[index] is O(1). Time complexity for access is constant; space is O(n) for storage. This connects to L4 as the prerequisite for element manipulation. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, and misunderstanding array mutability. The real pattern is declaring an array and using square brackets for access.

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15
Access arr[1] returns 10
Array has one element at index 0: 100

approaches.name: Extra Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Create a new array and copy elements to their rotated positions based on modulo indexing. This approach is simple and efficient but uses extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate Array Right by k Using Extra Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution that creates a new array and copies elements to rotated positions using modulo indexing.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that rotating an array right by k positions means each element moves to position (i + k) % n in the rotated array, where n is the array length. This works because when we shift right, elements wrap around to the beginning when they reach the end. The modulo operation handles this wrap-around behavior perfectly. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Get the length n of the input array. 2. Create a new result array of the same size n. 3. For each index i from 0 to n-1: calculate new_index = (i + k) % n, then copy arr[i] to result[new_index]. 4. Return the result array. Time complexity is O(n) since we iterate through the array once. Space complexity is O(n) for the new array. This connects directly to the full problem as it's the complete implementation. Common mistakes include: not handling k > n correctly (modulo fixes this), forgetting to create a new array and modifying the original, and off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: def rotate(arr, k): n = len(arr); result = [0]*n; for i in range(n): result[(i+k)%n] = arr[i]; return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Calculate Rotated Positions with Modulo
ladders.desc: Helper function that computes new index for each element when rotating right by k.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for any element at index i in the original array, after rotating right by k steps, its new position is (i + k) % n, where n is array length. This works because adding k shifts it right, and modulo n ensures wrap-around when the index exceeds n-1. Step-by-step: 1. Input: original index i, rotation steps k, array length n. 2. Compute new_index = (i + k) % n. 3. Return new_index. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the core calculation for placing elements in the new array. Common mistakes: forgetting modulo, using integer division instead of modulo, not handling negative k (but problem specifies right rotation). Real code pattern: def new_index(i, k, n): return (i + k) % n.

examples.trace: new_index = (0 + 2) % 5 = 2 % 5 = 2. Return 2.
new_index = (3 + 4) % 3 = 7 % 3 = 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mapping Original to Rotated Positions
ladders.desc: The central technique of creating a new array by copying each element to its computed rotated position.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that rotation can be achieved by building a new array where each element from the original is placed at a specific calculated position. This works because we know exactly where each element should end up after rotation. Step-by-step: 1. Create an empty result array of same size as input. 2. For each index i in original array: compute new position using (i + k) % n, then set result[new_position] = original[i]. 3. Return result. Time O(n) for the loop, space O(n) for result. This is the core of L0, combining the index calculation with array construction. Common mistakes: initializing result incorrectly, not iterating over all indices, or copying to wrong index. Real code pattern: result = [0]*len(arr); for i in range(len(arr)): result[(i+k)%len(arr)] = arr[i]; return result.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Circular Indexing
ladders.desc: Using modulo to handle wrap-around when indices go beyond array bounds.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that modulo (%) gives the remainder when dividing by n, which maps any integer to a value between 0 and n-1. This is perfect for circular arrays because when an index exceeds n-1, it wraps back to start. For example, in an array of size 5, index 5 wraps to 0, 6 to 1, etc. Step-by-step: given index i and modulus n, compute i % n. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling the new position calculation. Common mistakes: confusing modulo with division, not handling negative numbers (but indices are non-negative here), or using wrong modulus. Real code pattern: wrapped_index = i % n.

examples.trace: 7 % 5 = 2 because 7 divided by 5 is 1 remainder 2. Return 2.
10 % 3 = 1 because 10 divided by 3 is 3 remainder 1. Return 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Assignment
ladders.desc: Accessing and modifying elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access or modify any element using its index (position). Indexing starts at 0. For example, arr[0] is first element, arr[1] second, etc. Step-by-step: to read value at index i, use arr[i]; to assign value v to index i, use arr[i] = v. Time O(1) for access/assignment, space O(1). This feeds into L3 as it's used to place elements in the new array. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing, accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing assignment with comparison. Real code pattern: value = arr[index]; arr[index] = new_value.

examples.trace: Original arr = [10,20,30]. Assign arr[1] = 50. arr becomes [10,50,30]. Return [10,50,30].
Original arr = [5,6,7,8]. Assign arr[0] = 1. arr becomes [1,6,7,8]. Return [1,6,7,8].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Understanding what an array is and how to use indices to access elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements in order. Each element has a position called an index, starting from 0. For example, in array [10,20,30], index 0 has value 10, index 1 has 20, index 2 has 30. The intuition is that arrays allow quick access to any element if you know its index. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array with elements. 2. To get element at index i, look at the i-th position from start. 3. Arrays have a length (number of elements). Time O(1) to access, space O(n) to store. This is foundational for L4 as it introduces the basic idea. Common mistakes: thinking indices start at 1, confusing length with last index (last index is length-1), or trying to access non-existent indices. Real code pattern: arr = [value1, value2, ...]; first_element = arr[0]; length = len(arr).

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 10, index 2 = 15. Getting index 1 returns value 10.
Array has elements: index 0 = 100, index 1 = 200, index 2 = 300, index 3 = 400. Getting index 3 returns value 400.

approaches.name: Reverse Three Times O(n)
approaches.summary: Reverse the entire array, then reverse the first k elements and the remaining n-k elements separately. This achieves rotation in-place with constant extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate Array Right by K Using Reverse Three Times
ladders.desc: Complete in-place array rotation using three reverse operations
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires rotating an array to the right by k positions in-place with O(1) extra space. The reverse-three-times approach works because rotating an array right by k is equivalent to: 1) reversing the entire array, which puts the last k elements at the front but in reverse order, 2) reversing the first k elements to correct their order, and 3) reversing the remaining n-k elements to restore their original order. This elegant solution avoids using extra arrays and handles edge cases like k > n by using k % n. The intuition comes from observing that rotation preserves relative order within segments - we're essentially moving the last k elements to the front while maintaining their internal order. The algorithm: 1) Normalize k = k % n to handle rotations larger than array size, 2) Reverse entire array from 0 to n-1, 3) Reverse first k elements from 0 to k-1, 4) Reverse remaining n-k elements from k to n-1. Time complexity is O(n) since each reverse operation visits each element once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use constant extra variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete solution using the reverse subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to normalize k, incorrect index boundaries in reverse operations, and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: def rotate(nums, k): n = len(nums); k %= n; reverse(nums, 0, n-1); reverse(nums, 0, k-1); reverse(nums, k, n-1).

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7, k=3%7=3. Step 2: Reverse entire array [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] → [7,6,5,4,3,2,1]. Step 3: Reverse first k=3 elements [7,6,5,4,3,2,1] → [5,6,7,4,3,2,1]. Step 4: Reverse remaining n-k=4 elements from index 3 to 6: [5,6,7,4,3,2,1] → [5,6,7,1,2,3,4]. Final: [5,6,7,1,2,3,4].

Step 1: n=4, k=2%4=2. Step 2: Reverse entire array [-1,-100,3,99] → [99,3,-100,-1]. Step 3: Reverse first k=2 elements [99,3,-100,-1] → [3,99,-100,-1]. Step 4: Reverse remaining n-k=2 elements from index 2 to 3: [3,99,-100,-1] → [3,99,-1,-100]. Final: [3,99,-1,-100].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Array Segment In-Place
ladders.desc: Reverse a contiguous segment of an array between two indices
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the rotation approach. The reverse subroutine takes an array and two indices (start and end), then reverses all elements between those indices inclusive. The intuition is using two pointers that move toward each other, swapping elements until they meet in the middle. This works because reversing means the first element goes to last position, second to second-last, etc. - we can achieve this by swapping symmetric pairs. Algorithm: 1) Initialize left = start, right = end, 2) While left < right: swap array[left] and array[right], increment left, decrement right. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the number of elements in the segment (end-start+1). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use constant extra variables. This connects to L0 by being called three times in the complete solution. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < in the loop condition (which would swap middle element with itself unnecessarily), incorrect index calculations, and not handling empty segments. Real code pattern: def reverse(arr, start, end): while start < end: arr[start], arr[end] = arr[end], arr[start]; start += 1; end -= 1.

examples.trace: Initial: [1,2,3,4,5], left=1, right=3. Step 1: swap arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4 → [1,4,3,2,5], left=2, right=2. Loop ends as left=2, right=2 (not <). Final: [1,4,3,2,5].

Initial: [10,20,30,40,50,60], left=0, right=5. Step 1: swap arr[0]=10 and arr[5]=60 → [60,20,30,40,50,10], left=1, right=4. Step 2: swap arr[1]=20 and arr[4]=50 → [60,50,30,40,20,10], left=2, right=3. Step 3: swap arr[2]=30 and arr[3]=40 → [60,50,40,30,20,10], left=3, right=2. Loop ends as left=3, right=2 (not <). Final: [60,50,40,30,20,10].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-Reverse Pattern for Rotation
ladders.desc: Understanding why reversing three specific segments achieves rotation
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: rotating an array right by k can be achieved by three reverse operations. The intuition comes from mathematical observation: if we denote the array as A[0...n-1] and want to rotate right by k, the result should be last k elements followed by first n-k elements. Let's denote: first segment = A[0...n-k-1], second segment = A[n-k...n-1]. After rotation, we want: second segment + first segment. Now consider: 1) Reverse entire array: (second segment + first segment) becomes reversed(first segment) + reversed(second segment). 2) Reverse first k elements (which are reversed(second segment)): becomes second segment. 3) Reverse remaining n-k elements (which are reversed(first segment)): becomes first segment. Thus we get: second segment + first segment = rotated array. The key insight is that reversal changes order but preserves elements - by strategically reversing segments, we can reposition groups of elements. This connects to L1 by explaining why we call reverse three times with specific indices. Common mistakes include misunderstanding which segments to reverse (first k vs last k), not seeing the mathematical proof, and confusing left vs right rotation. Real code pattern: The conceptual pattern without implementation details: rotation_right(arr, k) = reverse(all) then reverse(first k) then reverse(last n-k).

examples.trace: Original: [A,B,C,D,E]. Desired: last 2 elements (D,E) then first 3 (A,B,C) = [D,E,A,B,C]. Proof: 1) Reverse all: [E,D,C,B,A] = reversed(last2) + reversed(first3) = [E,D] + [C,B,A]. 2) Reverse first k=2: [D,E,C,B,A]. 3) Reverse remaining n-k=3: [D,E,A,B,C]. Matches desired.

Original: [1,2,3,4,5,6]. Desired: last 4 (3,4,5,6) then first 2 (1,2) = [3,4,5,6,1,2]. Proof: 1) Reverse all: [6,5,4,3,2,1] = reversed(last4) + reversed(first2) = [6,5,4,3] + [2,1]. 2) Reverse first k=4: [3,4,5,6,2,1]. 3) Reverse remaining n-k=2: [3,4,5,6,1,2]. Matches desired.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic for K Normalization
ladders.desc: Handling k values larger than array size using modulo operation
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial because rotating an array of size n by k where k ≥ n is equivalent to rotating by k % n. The intuition comes from periodic behavior: rotating an array n times brings it back to original, so rotation is cyclic with period n. Mathematically, position i after rotation right by k becomes (i + k) % n in the new array (with appropriate indexing). Therefore, k and k + n produce same result. Algorithm: given array of length n and rotation k, compute k = k % n. If n = 0 (empty array), no operation needed. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by ensuring the three-reverse pattern works correctly for all k values. Common mistakes include not handling n=0 case, forgetting modulo when k > n, and misunderstanding that k % n works for negative k (in Python, % returns non-negative remainder). Real code pattern: if n > 0: k = k % n else: k = 0.

examples.trace: Array has 5 elements. Rotating 7 right is same as rotating 7 % 5 = 2 right because: 7 = 5 + 2. Rotating 5 times gives original array, then 2 more gives net rotation of 2. Calculation: 7 ÷ 5 = 1 remainder 2, so 7 % 5 = 2.

Array has 3 elements. Rotating 12 right is same as rotating 12 % 3 = 0 right because: 12 is multiple of 3 (4 × 3 = 12). Rotating 3 times gives original, rotating 6, 9, or 12 times also gives original. Calculation: 12 ÷ 3 = 4 remainder 0, so 12 % 3 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Two Array Elements
ladders.desc: Exchange values at two indices in an array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the reverse subroutine. Swapping means exchanging the values at two positions i and j in an array. The intuition is simple: we need a temporary variable to hold one value while we overwrite it. Algorithm: 1) Store arr[i] in temp, 2) Set arr[i] = arr[j], 3) Set arr[j] = temp. Alternatively in Python: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) for the temporary variable. This connects to L3 as it's the core operation used in modular arithmetic implementation (though indirectly - swapping is used in reverse which uses normalized k). Common mistakes include overwriting without temporary variable (arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = arr[i] loses original arr[i]), using incorrect indices, and not handling cases where i == j (swap works but unnecessary). Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp.

examples.trace: Initial: [5,6,7]. Step 1: temp = arr[0] = 5. Step 2: arr[0] = arr[2] = 7 → [7,6,7]. Step 3: arr[2] = temp = 5 → [7,6,5]. Final: [7,6,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Length
ladders.desc: Understanding array indices, length, and accessing elements
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: arrays are ordered collections where each element has an index starting from 0. The length n is the number of elements. Valid indices range from 0 to n-1. Accessing arr[i] retrieves element at position i. The intuition is like house numbering on a street: first house is number 0, second is 1, etc. This foundational knowledge is essential because all rotation operations depend on correct indexing. Key points: 1) Index 0 is first element, index n-1 is last element, 2) Length n = number of elements, 3) Empty array has n=0 and no valid indices, 4) Accessing invalid index (i < 0 or i ≥ n) causes error. This connects to L4 as swapping requires valid indices. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (thinking index n is valid), confusing length with last index, and not understanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, len(arr) gives length, arr[0] accesses first element, arr[len(arr)-1] accesses last element.

examples.trace: Array has 4 elements: index 0 = 10, index 1 = 20, index 2 = 30, index 3 = 40. Length n = 4. Valid indices: 0,1,2,3. Invalid indices: -1,4,5 etc. Last element is at index n-1 = 3, value 40.
Array has 1 element: index 0 = 5. Length n = 1. Valid indices: only 0. Invalid indices: -1,1,2 etc. Last element is at index n-1 = 0, value 5.

approaches.name: GCD Cyclic Replacements O(n)
approaches.summary: Use the greatest common divisor (GCD) of n and k to perform cyclic swaps in groups, moving each element directly to its final position. This is an in-place method with optimal time and space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Rotate Array Right by k Using GCD Cyclic Replacements
ladders.desc: Complete in-place array rotation using GCD-based cyclic swaps.
ladders.explanation: This approach rotates an array to the right by k steps in O(n) time and O(1) space by leveraging the greatest common divisor (GCD) of n (array length) and k. The intuition is that when rotating an array, elements move in cycles whose length is determined by GCD(n, k). By identifying these cycles, we can perform direct swaps to place each element in its final position without extra storage. The algorithm works by iterating through GCD(n, k) starting positions. For each start index i from 0 to gcd-1, we initiate a cycle: store the element at i, then repeatedly move elements from current position j to (j+k) mod n until returning to i, placing the stored element at the last position. This ensures every element is moved exactly once. Time complexity is O(n) as each element is visited once, and space is O(1) aside from a few variables. Common mistakes include not handling k > n (use k % n), incorrect cycle termination, and off-by-one errors in index calculations. This method is efficient and elegant, avoiding the need for reversal or extra arrays.

examples.trace: n=6, k=2, gcd(6,2)=2. Start cycles: i=0: store arr[0]=1, j=0, next=(0+2)%6=2, move arr[2]=3 to arr[0]=[3,2,3,4,5,6], j=2, next=(2+2)%6=4, move arr[4]=5 to arr[2]=[3,2,5,4,5,6], j=4, next=(4+2)%6=0 (back to start), place stored 1 at arr[4]=[3,2,5,4,1,6]. i=1: store arr[1]=2, j=1, next=(1+2)%6=3, move arr[3]=4 to arr[1]=[3,4,5,4,1,6], j=3, next=(3+2)%6=5, move arr[5]=6 to arr[3]=[3,4,5,6,1,6], j=5, next=(5+2)%6=1, place stored 2 at arr[5]=[3,4,5,6,1,2]. Final: [5,6,1,2,3,4] after adjusting indices? Wait, trace shows [3,4,5,6,1,2] but correct is [5,6,1,2,3,4]. Let's correct: Actually, after i=0 cycle: start arr=[1,2,3,4,5,6], store temp=1, j=0, next=2, move arr[2]=3 to arr[0]=[3,2,3,4,5,6], j=2, next=4, move arr[4]=5 to arr[2]=[3,2,5,4,5,6], j=4, next=0, place temp=1 at arr[4]=[3,2,5,4,1,6]. i=1: store temp=2, j=1, next=3, move arr[3]=4 to arr[1]=[3,4,5,4,1,6], j=3, next=5, move arr[5]=6 to arr[3]=[3,4,5,6,1,6], j=5, next=1, place temp=2 at arr[5]=[3,4,5,6,1,2]. This gives [3,4,5,6,1,2], but target is [5,6,1,2,3,4]. Error: Need to rotate right by k=2, so final indices should shift. Let's redo with correct logic: For right rotation, element at i moves to (i+k)%n. In cyclic replacement, we move backward: start from i, store arr[i], then move arr[(i-k)%n] to arr[i], etc. But standard GCD method does left rotation. For right rotation by k, we can do left rotation by n-k. So k=2, n=6, left rotate by 4. gcd(6,4)=2. i=0: store arr[0]=1, j=0, next=(0+4)%6=4, move arr[4]=5 to arr[0]=[5,2,3,4,5,6], j=4, next=(4+4)%6=2, move arr[2]=3 to arr[4]=[5,2,3,4,3,6], j=2, next=(2+4)%6=0, place stored 1 at arr[2]=[5,2,1,4,3,6]. i=1: store arr[1]=2, j=1, next=(1+4)%6=5, move arr[5]=6 to arr[1]=[5,6,1,4,3,6], j=5, next=(5+4)%6=3, move arr[3]=4 to arr[5]=[5,6,1,4,3,4], j=3, next=(3+4)%6=1, place stored 2 at arr[3]=[5,6,1,2,3,4]. Final: [5,6,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Perform Cyclic Replacements for One Start Index
ladders.desc: Execute a single cycle of element swaps starting from a given index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles one cycle in the GCD cyclic replacements method. Given an array arr, its length n, rotation step k, and a start index start, it moves elements in a cycle where each element jumps k positions to the right (with wrap-around). The intuition is that starting from start, we store the element at start, then repeatedly move the element from position (current + k) % n to the current position, updating current to (current + k) % n, until we return to start, where we place the stored element. This ensures all elements in that cycle are correctly rotated. The algorithm: 1. Store temp = arr[start]. 2. Set current = start. 3. Loop: compute next = (current + k) % n. If next == start, break and place temp at arr[current]. Else, move arr[next] to arr[current], set current = next. 4. After loop, arr[current] = temp. Time complexity for one cycle is O(n/gcd) in worst case, but overall across all cycles sums to O(n). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include infinite loops if not checking for cycle completion, incorrect index arithmetic, and not handling the final placement. This sub-routine is called for each start index from 0 to gcd-1 in the full solution.

examples.trace: start=0, temp=arr[0]=1. current=0, next=(0+2)%6=2, move arr[2]=3 to arr[0]=[3,2,3,4,5,6], current=2. next=(2+2)%6=4, move arr[4]=5 to arr[2]=[3,2,5,4,5,6], current=4. next=(4+2)%6=0 (==start), break, place temp=1 at arr[4]=[3,2,5,4,1,6]. Final arr after this cycle: [3,2,5,4,1,6].

start=0, temp=10. current=0, next=(0+1)%4=1, move arr[1]=20 to arr[0]=[20,20,30,40], current=1. next=(1+1)%4=2, move arr[2]=30 to arr[1]=[20,30,30,40], current=2. next=(2+1)%4=3, move arr[3]=40 to arr[2]=[20,30,40,40], current=3. next=(3+1)%4=0 (==start), break, place temp=10 at arr[3]=[20,30,40,10]. Final: [20,30,40,10].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using GCD to Determine Number of Cycles
ladders.desc: Calculate GCD of n and k to find how many independent cycles exist.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that when rotating an array of length n by k steps, the elements move in cycles, and the number of such cycles is equal to the greatest common divisor (GCD) of n and k. This happens because the rotation operation forms a cyclic group: starting from an index i, repeatedly adding k modulo n will eventually return to i after LCM(n,k)/k steps, and the cycle length is n/GCD(n,k). The number of disjoint cycles is GCD(n,k), as each cycle starts from indices 0, 1, ..., GCD(n,k)-1. For example, if n=6 and k=2, GCD=2, so there are 2 cycles: one starting at index 0 and one at index 1. This reduces the problem to performing GCD cycles, each handled by the cyclic replacement sub-routine. The algorithm: 1. Compute g = gcd(n, k). 2. For i from 0 to g-1, perform a cycle starting at i. This ensures all elements are rotated correctly in O(n) time. Time complexity for GCD computation is O(log min(n,k)) using Euclidean algorithm, and the cycles take O(n). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not reducing k modulo n first, misunderstanding cycle count, or assuming cycles are always of length n. This logic is key to the full solution's efficiency.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(6,2): 6 % 2 = 0, so gcd=2. Number of cycles = 2. Cycle starts: i=0 and i=1. Each cycle will rotate elements: for i=0, cycle includes indices 0,2,4; for i=1, indices 1,3,5.
Compute gcd(4,3): 4 % 3 = 1, 3 % 1 = 0, so gcd=1. Number of cycles = 1. Cycle starts at i=0 only, covering all indices 0,1,2,3 in one cycle.

testCases.expected: gcd = 2 (but n=0 edge case, assume n>0)

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Find the GCD of two integers using the Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers a and b is the largest positive integer that divides both without remainder. For the cyclic replacements approach, we need GCD to determine the number of cycles. The Euclidean algorithm is an efficient method: repeatedly replace the larger number by its remainder when divided by the smaller, until one number is zero; the other is the GCD. Intuition: GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b) because any common divisor of a and b also divides a mod b. Algorithm: 1. While b != 0: set temp = b, b = a % b, a = temp. 2. Return a. Time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) as the numbers reduce quickly. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite loops if not updating variables correctly, and edge cases like a=0 or b=0. In array rotation, we use GCD(n, k) with n>0 and k non-negative. This building block feeds into the core logic for cycle determination.

examples.trace: a=6, b=2. b != 0: temp = 2, b = 6 % 2 = 0, a = 2. Now b=0, return a=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic for Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Compute (i + k) % n to handle wrap-around in circular arrays.
ladders.explanation: In array rotation, when moving an element k steps to the right, if the index exceeds n-1, it should wrap around to the beginning. Modular arithmetic provides this wrap-around: (i + k) % n gives the new index, where % is the modulo operator returning the remainder after division. Intuition: Think of the array as a circle; adding k and taking modulo n ensures we stay within 0 to n-1. For example, if n=5 and i=4, k=2, then (4+2)%5 = 6%5 = 1, wrapping from end to start. Algorithm: new_index = (old_index + k) % n. Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes include using integer division instead of modulo, off-by-one errors, or not handling negative k (use ((i+k) % n + n) % n for negative). This operation is fundamental in the cyclic replacement sub-routine for calculating next positions.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Access and modify elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index starting from 0. For an array arr of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing an element: value = arr[i] retrieves the element at index i. Modifying an element: arr[i] = new_value sets the element at index i to new_value. Intuition: Think of the array as a list of boxes numbered from 0; you can look inside or change the content of any box by its number. This is fundamental because in rotation, we need to read and write elements at specific positions. Time complexity for access/modify is O(1). Space is O(1) per operation. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds (e.g., arr[n] which causes errors), confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing, or not updating variables correctly during swaps. This foundation supports all higher-level operations in the ladder.

examples.input: arr = [10, 20, 30], access index 1, modify index 2 to 40
examples.trace: arr = [10, 20, 30]. Access arr[1]: value = 20. Modify arr[2] = 40: arr becomes [10, 20, 40].

examples.input: arr = [5, 6, 7, 8], access index 0, modify index 3 to 9
examples.trace: arr = [5, 6, 7, 8]. Access arr[0]: value = 5. Modify arr[3] = 9: arr becomes [5, 6, 7, 9].
